speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sometimes I can't sleep as I remember. [INAUDIBLE] it comes back. You never can forget anything like that in your life. It's always there. Seems like you can't forget your parents. You can't forget anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was seven months old and she was [? throwing out ?] there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you? You'd been beaten just before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And when she died, I became very ill. First of all, emotion. Second, I had typhus. Another one too, I had typhus. I had typhus. And I had an allergy. It was awful, with different kinds of things on my body. I was blowing up. I looked like a nine-month-old pregnant woman. I was thin. My head was swollen. My neck was swollen. I had no hair. I looked dreadful. And I felt dreadful. I felt like I would die tomorrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that went on with me quite a few days, a week. I can't remember how many times. And one morning, it was beautiful sunshine. I wasn't-- the snow was not there anymore. And an English Rabbi came in. And he said to me, are you Edith Polgar? [INAUDIBLE] or Edith Spielmann? And I said, yes. Who are you? He was in a-- he was not in a Rabbi. He was in a-- he was like a soldier. And he said, why do you ask me? He said, your sisters want to see you. When did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before we get to liberation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you-- you were in Bergen-Belsen and your child died. And then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: About another two or three weeks before the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she died fairly late in the war, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you in hospital for that whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you look after yourself then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just lying there. And crawled at night time to get some water wash myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bergen-Belsen? Bergen-Belsen was a huge, huge place, bigger as Auschwitz. Really, I can't say. There was all kind of nations there who was in politic, too, not only Jews. There were Russian, Czechoslovakian, Yugoslavia, any nation. You name it, they were there. They were better off than we were, but they still were in a very bad state. And I haven't seen much of it, because I was very sick. And when my child died, I was emotionally so sick I couldn't care less if I see the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you going then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My willpower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, why did you have-- why would you have willpower? You've lost all your family. You've lost your child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to see my sisters. And I want to see my husband. Because I knew my parents were not alive anymore. That kept me on going.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know anybody in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. Nobody. I didn't have anybody who I knew there. Only-- I mean, you talk to people there when you are there. But you didn't know-- this came from here and there. But I wasn't interested at all. Because you were so much down that you were not interested [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did people do when Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody worked. They died of hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- did it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or they hit them. Or, they did a lot of things, torture them. That's what they did in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there no food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same food in Auschwitz, exactly the same food. And if they took them to work, it was such a hard work-- the drill, but there was no drilling. Because they did it was their hands. And people were dying every day by hundreds, because they didn't have any energy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anybody leave Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. How could they? They didn't. Nobody had a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, they didn't take people to work camps or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Once they tried. Once. Somebody tried. And that one they caught him, and they shot hundreds of them. They said to teach [INAUDIBLE] lesson, so don't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who did you speak to if you were in a camp with people from all different nations and you had been by yourself? Do you remember having relationships with other people there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. You didn't have with anybody, because you were not strong enough. You were happy if you could go out to the toilet and wash yourself and go back to bed and give you a chance to sleep if they didn't throw you out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, describe the liberation to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I started to telling you, that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the English Rabbi came by, asked me who I am and if I am there. And like I told you, in my childhood, I learned to speak English. But English to speak there and English to speak with somebody who knows a language is a different thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I could understand him. And he could understand me. And then he told me if I want to see my sisters. And I said, my sisters? Are they alive? Really? He said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It will take three days before you see them. Well, I thought, that's not true. He didn't tell me anything. We said-- I said, I can't believe you. He said, you believe me. I wouldn't tell you. But I couldn't sleep, not day and not night. And I washed myself. And that cleared myself. And I did everything. The time was so long, it wasn't true. So he came back to me. And he took me in Bergen-Belsen to the hospital. And that hospital was called [? Tannenwald, ?] because it was next to Bergen-Belsen. And they put me into a hospital where all the tuberculosis were. Because it was a lung-- it was a lung section, tuberculosis--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tuberculosis.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the lung section. Because they thought I had that, because I was small, and I couldn't talk. I was in a dreadful state. And he came back. I couldn't talk. I couldn't do anything. I had a little hair, maybe it was one inch long. They asked a nurse there, one of the German nurses. I said, give me a ribbon that I can put it on my head. She laughed, and the others laughed. I always had my sense of humor. I never gave up. I had a horrible-- I don't know horrible willpower. It's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to me, you have to wait and you get well. You will see your sisters, because I know you will. I said, tell me. He said to me, I won't tell you anything. So I sat there, I did [? there. ?] One morning, he came back. I said, you're coming to see me. He said, yes. Now you will see your sisters. So it came by. It was morning, it was midday. It was afternoon. It was night time. The next morning, there was nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: About lunchtime, he brought my two sisters. You can imagine. I didn't let myself kiss from my two sisters, because I thought I was very sick-- not to give it to them. I can't tell you the reunion in words, emotion, what was there. I don't want to cry now, because I feel I should tell you everything. And she said to me [INAUDIBLE]. We'll take you away from here. So they went away again. And again, hours and hours, they didn't came back. Because see, I wasn't in a state such that they can take me away from there. Because they had to sign if I die in the meantime, that they're taking me. They had to sign [? there ?] a possibility it should be. Because they came with an American Rabbi and with an ambulance car that he organized from Lippstadt with a few American soldiers to pick me up. That was my middle sister. She was very clever, [INAUDIBLE]. And it was around 5 o'clock, 6 o'clock at night, that it must have been. They came back with all the papers. They took me to wash me, put something on me, and took me down. My sisters signs the papers, both of them. I had to sign it, too. And they took me back to Lippstadt to a very good sanatorium in Lippstadt. I was hours in the ambulance. [INAUDIBLE] it was about four or five hours, maybe six hours away from there. And when I was arrived, I was nearly unconscious. I couldn't remember on that until the next morning. They were standing beside me. And I was in bedroom in luxury. By that time, there's a lot of doctors around me. And I was there for about four months until I became that I could walk again, because I was in wheelchair. I was on crutches before I could walk again. So they came and picked me up. And we were living in Lippstadt for a few weeks before I became-- that we could travel. We [? paired ?] up the railway to took us back to Hungary with my sisters together. And we arrived after three weeks in Budapest to the [? welfare ?]. There was a [? welfare ?]. I had a girlfriend who was a doctor, Margaret Stein, and she was a working at the [? welfare ?] at that time. Now, we arrived. And we were hungry and dirty. And you can imagine how we looked. And she saw me. She recognized me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't recognize her. And she said to me, do you know your husband is alive? I said, what?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, yes. He was already four times here to have a look if you had arrived or what, because there was another Edith Spielmann-- she died-- that was in another village. So we got a nice room. We got some clothes. We got some food. We had hair. We went to the hair dresser. We got some money from them. And in three days' time, she came back to me. [INAUDIBLE] And she said, I have somebody to see you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she wanted-- I wanted to go straight where my husband is. She said, no. You can't, because you are not allowed. It was like a quarantine, you know? So after three days, she came to our room. And she said, do you want to see your husband? And my husband came. And that's how we met each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are 5,000 Jews in Gyor. And there was five people who met each as after the war with their husband. And I was amongst them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five people, five couple only who met again after the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of 5,000?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of 5,000. And I was amongst one of them. That's how we met again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you saw your husband again for the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were crying, of course. Everybody was crying. And emotionally, it was shocking. And he said, don't cry. I got everything ready for you, what you ever wanted and what we ever had. He already had a home. He already had a shop. He was already working for himself. He took in my two sisters. We had a beautiful flat. He bought a unit. It was already in everything that you wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: He bought a unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: He bought.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how-- how long had he been back?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had been back for a year. And from his side, his mother's, his mother had quite a few things that he sold. And he bought it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had happened to him during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] he was in Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the whole war?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came back from there. He had three typhus, too. He was very sick. But he managed to get back. And that's how we met. And then our life started again. And when life started again, I married off my two sisters. They had children. I had a child, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your child born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gyor, Judy was born in Gyor.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: What date in '46?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 23. We were very, very happy for quite a while. Everybody was happy. Everybody started again to come together and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had a shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: What shop? What sort of shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: A textile shop. And we were very happy. Judy was born and everything. And when the children, my two sisters had a boy, [INAUDIBLE]. My Greta had [INAUDIBLE]. Herta had Peter. And we went-- we were very close. Cousins, they went to school together. They were about six years old when Judy came home from school and the others, too. And they said, you bloody Jew in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is about 1952 then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then when that was happened, we couldn't do anything. But we said, if the first opportunity to get out from here, we go. Well, the first opportunity was when the Hungarian revolution was. I went both to my sisters and said, through the night, at 12 o'clock somebody will pick you up, to my other sister, too. We paid a bus from town hall. We left the key. Everybody left the key in the flat. The kids got a little bag in each bag on the back. If anything happened to us that we couldn't go through, we said that we had arranged that who will take the kids. But luckily, we were very lucky. We escaped very well. And we arrived the next morning in Vienna. And I never look back. I never have been in Hungary. I have only have been once in Austria, because I needed some documents from there. And I have never been back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ho-- And you left everything behind in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. We arrived in Australia every couple with $2.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to go far away from Europe, as far as I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got to Vienna, did you know where you'd be going to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked on the Australian [? council. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they got to Austria. And they wanted me to stay there for a while to be a [? talmadge ?] there.

speaker: interviewer
text: To be--

speaker: interviewee
text: A [? talmadge. ?] To intro-- how you call it-- to introduce people and to interpret from Hungary to English. And then I went out to say my husband, my whole family, oh, I have a good job. We're staying here. We can afford to stay here. And they said oh, no. So with the first plane was provided from here to fly out to Australia, we were amongst the first people to fly out with a little small French plane. Because that time, we had to go around the Suez Canal. Because that time, there was the Suez Canal war in Israel. And they wouldn't allow Jew goes through there. So we flew a lot, much more-- twice as much than any other normal people to fly. We were sick on the plane. We were-- it was shocking. And the children were crying and everything. Anyhow, when we arrived here, we had the red carpet treatment. It was a red carpet treatment and on the floor with a band playing the Hungarian [? hymnals. ?] But I hated from every minute of it. And we went to [? Bougain ?] [? Bougainvillea? ?] [? Bougainvillea-- ?] to the camp. We were there for, I think, for a week. And I had some relations here. And they brought us out. And we went to Greenwich. Greenwich was one of the welfares organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish welfare organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish welfare organizations who had a house there. And all the refugees, some of them they put there. And we were there about for six months, I'd say. I only was there for three months, because I wanted to get out from there. And I met somebody who gave me a job and gave my husband a job. And-- but before, we went out-- we went to Greenwich-- I forgot to tell you that I just remember when we arrived in Darwin-- no, when we arrived in Karachi, the plane went down. Because he had to [? fool ?] the w-- [INAUDIBLE]. And we were there for a week-- for a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Karachi. And I was pregnant that time when I came out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pregnant again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again. And I lost it in Karachi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many months pregnant were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three months. And I was bleeding, so they took me there to hospital in Karachi. And I was there over the night. And the next day, they picked me up from there. On the plane, I was very, very ill. So I arrived actually here before I went to [? Bougainvillea. ?] I after the Royal Women's Hospital, they have a [INAUDIBLE]. And after that, I couldn't walk again, because I was very weak. And in [? Bougainvillea ?] when I was, that's why my relation came out. And we went to Greenwich. And from Greenwich, I went back to the hospital, because I was very weak. And then from the hospital back to Greenwich, and then we started [INAUDIBLE] out life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your impressions of Australia when you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved every minute of it. Only when we arrived in Darwin, and I said, oh, it's so hot here. How will I work there? I couldn't-- I don't know how we'll stand it. But I have to. I have to. I have to. And when I arrived in Sydney, we were all very happy. And that's how we started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you used to talk to people about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Well, first of all, there were [INAUDIBLE]. A lot of people came. And we all had said-- most of the people had the same experience who had experienced what I had. Only, I had a worse one than anybody else, because of the child. But I talked to a lot of people. And we were discussing it. And then after a while, you had to live your own life. Because you have to-- how should I say-- you have to establish yourself to make your living. And my darling late husband, in between us was 22 years different. So I was the young one. I had to work more than him. And Judy was already nine years old. And we had to work to do something. So we don't-- didn't have time to social function or socializing with anybody. Because I worked seven days and seven nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I was lucky. I had my millinery experience. I worked in [INAUDIBLE] for five years in a shop. And my husband worked at the post office. So that time, it was excellent. Because one wages you could put away. And the other one, we lived on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we were established after five years, I went to another shop to work, a millinery in Bondi Junction for another three years. And then I had enough money to buy a shop. And that's how I started my work. What shop did you buy?

speaker: interviewee
text: A frock shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your frock shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auburn. And there I was for 12 or 14 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you living by this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bondi Junction. Always lived here in Bondi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your sisters here, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They both-- one lived in Canberra and one lived it was here in Bondi. And my two sisters had a shop established themselves in Kempsey. They were partners together. So that's how we started our life. And I can only say God bless Australia, because there is no any other country in the world like Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In every way. First of all, the country, it's sunshine. It's big. It's beautiful. The people are beautiful in it. If you want to work, you can do anything what you want so long as you're not cheating and you don't do any fraud. And if anybody wants to work, any youngsters or any old ones, you can always do what you want. It's the most beautiful country in the world. I wish I would be born here. That would be the nicest way to do. But my daughter is already like she would have been born here. My grandchildren have been born here. So I hope we never have to go away anywhere else so long as we live.

speaker: interviewer
text: I might cut there.
